questions.prompt: Grammar — Now or habit? "My father usually drives to work, but today he ___ the train."
questions.explanation: "today" marks a temporary action against the usual habit, so use the present continuous: today he is taking the train. "usually drives" is the habit (simple), but "today" switches us to the continuous; "takes"/"take" are simple, and "are taking" uses the plural be with he.

questions.prompt: Vocabulary — Choose the opposite: "This suitcase isn't heavy at all — in fact it's very ___."
questions.explanation: The opposite of heavy is light, so a case that isn't heavy is very light. Big, old and expensive are real adjectives, but none is the opposite of heavy, so they don't fit the contrast the sentence builds.

questions.prompt: Spelling — Choose the correct superlative: "That was the ___ day of my whole holiday."
questions.explanation: happy ends in consonant + y, so the superlative changes y to i and adds -est: the happiest day. "happyest" keeps the y wrongly, "most happy" treats a short -y adjective as long (it isn't), and "happier" is the comparative (for two things).

questions.prompt:
Reading — Read: "Last Saturday Omar and his friends played football in the park. After the game they were thirsty, so they bought cold drinks."
How did the friends feel after the game?
questions.explanation: The text says "they were thirsty", which is why they bought cold drinks, so (a) is correct. Read for the exact detail: cold (b) describes the drinks, not the boys, and bored (c) and afraid (d) are never mentioned in the passage.

questions.prompt: Grammar — Quantifier: "Hurry up! We don't have ___ time before the bus leaves."
questions.explanation: time is uncountable, so its amount word is much, and in a negative it fits naturally: we don't have much time. many and a few need countable plural nouns, and some is normally used in affirmatives, not negatives.

questions.prompt: Grammar — Future: "A: There's no milk left for the coffee. B: Oh, I ___ buy some on my way home." (deciding as you speak)
questions.explanation: B reacts to the news and decides at that moment, so use will + base verb: I will buy some. "am going to" would suggest a plan made earlier, "will to" wrongly adds to after will, and "going to" alone is missing the be (am).
